scopecontent.p: Autograph envelope of 25 May 1866. Portsmouth. This envelope is addressed to "Miss Hogarth, 6 South Pickwick Place, Hyde Park, London, W."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph envelope of 14 October 1868. Liverpool [?]. This envelope, addressed to Georgina Hogarth at 26 Wellington Street, London, and forwarded to Gad's Hill Place, perhaps contained the letter of 13 October 1868, published in Nonesuch III, page 672.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph envelope, signed, of 29 October 1868. Liverpool. This envelope, addressed to Georgina Hogarth at Gad's Hill Place, perhaps contained the letter published in Nonesuch III, page 676.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph envelope, signed, of 21 February 1869. Edinburgh. On the back of this envelope, which is addressed to Georgina Hogarth at the All the Year Round Office, Dickens writes: "Tell the Bootmaker to send that other boot here when done."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Abraham Holroyd. Autograph letter, signed, of 20 October 1858, containing approximately 60 words. Birmingham. Dickens thanks Holroyd for "the two little books" and will "have great pleasure in receiving the remainder of the series."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Thomas Hood. Autograph letter, signed, of 1 January 1844 [?], containing approximately 35 words. London [?]. In this letter, the top half of which is missing, Dickens thanks Hood for his "kind and charming notice" of A Christmas Carol , and indicates his willingness to "carol again when your magazine [ Hood's Magazine ] is a twelvemonth old." Published in substance in Pilgrim IV, page 1.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 5 December 1845, published in Pilgrim IV, page 447.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 1 July 1849, published in Nonesuch II, page 159.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 12 August 1849, published in Nonesuch II, page 167.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 17 April 1851, containing approximately 50 words. London. Dickens thanks Horne for flowers sent and sympathy expressed upon the death of his daughter Dora.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 6 April 1852, published in Nonesuch II, pages 385-386.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 18 April 1852, published in Nonesuch II, pages 387-388.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 30 December 1865, published in Nonesuch III, pages 449-450.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph envelope, signed, of an unknown date. London. This envelope, apparently sent by messenger, is addressed to Horne at "College Road, Chalk Farm Lane, Hampstead Road."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To "House." Autograph document, signed, of 1 July 1861, containing approximately 10 words. London. A cheque for five pounds, seventeen shillings payable through Messrs. Coutts and Co. to "House."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To "House and Wages." Autograph document, signed, of 18 April 1864, containing approximately 10 words. London. A cheque for ten pounds, ten shillings payable through Messrs.. Coutts and Co. to "House and Wages."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Robert Hunt. Autograph letter, signed, of 19 June 1855, containing approximately 105 words. London. Dickens believes that the proprietors of the Daily News will assist a former employee, Dr. Doogood, "in his unhappy circumstances."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To John Hunter. Autograph letter, signed, of 9 March 1860, containing approximately 80 words. London. Dickens reports that he has referred Hunter's letter to the author of an article where reference was made to insanitary kneading in bakehouses.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To William Blanchard Jerrold. Autograph envelope, signed, of 26 November 1858. London. This envelope is addressed to Jerrold at "Fairfield Farm, near Broadstairs, Kent."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To William Blanchard Jerrold. Autograph letter, signed, of 26 November 1858, containing approximately 30 words. Dickens encloses pieces he wrote, evidently on Douglas Jerrold. "Do what you like with them and consider them yours, not mine." The articles are not present.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Mrs. Joll. Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 15 May 1849, containing approximately 110 words. Dickens refuses an unspecified request and expresses disapproval of Mrs. Joll's practice of "republishing portions of the works of living writers without their consent."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Samuel Joseph. Autograph letter, signed, of 27 December 1841, published in Pilgrim II, page 452.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Edward Kaub. Autograph letter, signed, of 13 January 1854, containing approximately 120 words. London. Dickens has arranged for "Edward" to be seen by the Duchess of Sutherland, evidently about domestic employment.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To H. Kay-Shuttleworth. Autograph letter, signed, of 5 October 1848, published in substance in Nonesuch II, page 123. In Nonesuch the correspondent is identified as J. P. Kay-Shuttleworth.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope of 22 September 1845, published in Pilgrim IV, pages 384-385.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope of 10 October 1847, containing approximately 25 words. London. Dickens will see her on Tuesday at two o'clock.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 12 November 1847, containing approximately 100 words. London. Dickens writes: "We want to read a play at your theatre on Tuesday next." Instead of ten shillings per night for rehearsals, he insists on paying one pound, "which anybody will consider little enough."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 24 May 1848, containing approximately 310 words. London. Dickens approves of Miss Kenworthy's being accompanied by her father on the forthcoming theatrical tour. He outlines the schedule the actress and her escort will follow, proposes to pay the railroad fares for both of them, and in addition offers ten pounds to cover their expenses of board and lodging on the tour. Miss Kelly is asked to serve as intermediary in the negotiations.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 27 May 1848, containing approximately 130 words. Birmingham. Dickens sends a list of Miss Kenworthy's calls for rehearsals in Manchester, Liverpool, and Birmingham. "My brother Fred will see about her railroad fares, and will communicate with you therefore."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 20 June 1848, containing approximately 110 words. London. Dickens outlines the next month's schedule for his theatrical group. "We shall want to rehearse a condensation of Love, Law, and Physic at your threatre next Friday evening at 7."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 11 July 1848, containing approximately 40 words. London. Dickens writes: "Rehearsals of Used Up , on Thursday and Friday Evenings at 7 o'clock. Miss Kenworthy and her father to be ready for going to Scotland, at 1/2 past on 9 on Saturday morning."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 11 September 1848, containing approximately 200 words, summarized and published in part in Nonesuch II, page 119. Broadstairs, Kent. Dickens congratulates her on the "Manchester engagement" and advises her on using it to advantage in her career.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 3 September 1867, containing approximately 240 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens denies a rumor that he is ill. He sends printer's proofs "of the little things I told you I had written for a children's magazine at Boston." The proofs, of A Holiday Romance , are not present.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 2 October 1868, containing approximately 40 words. London. Dickens thanks Kent warmly for "those earnest and affecting words."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 1 August 1852, published in Nonesuch II, page 405.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 14 March 1860, containing approximately 65 words. London. Dickens must leave town on business for All the Year Round . He will communicate with Knight when he returns. The complimentary close and signature are cut out of this letter.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph envelope, signed, of an unknown date. London. This envelope, apparently sent by messenger, is addressed to Knight at "3 Clifton Road, ST. John's Wood, N.W."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To David Laing. Autograph letter, signed, of 15 April 1854, containing approximately 285 words. London. Dickens stresses his lifelong concern with "neglected youth in connection with our penal system." He expresses reluctance about seeking a seat in Parliament--"I believe I am more useful in my present place."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Robert Lamond. Autograph letter, signed, of 10 August 1848, published in Nonesuch II, page 116.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Charles Lanman. Autograph letter, signed, of 5 February 1868, published in Nonesuch III, pages 616-617.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 9 January 1852, published in substance in Nonesuch II, page 369.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 22 November 1852, published in substance in Nonesuch II, page 432.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 28 April 1852, published in substance in Nonesuch II, page 390. A fire-fragment with thirty-two words surviving.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph envelope, signed, of an unknown date. London. This envelope, apparently sent by messenger, is addressed to Lemon at "11 Gordon Street."

did.unittitle: G223: To Messrs. Letts and Sons

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Messrs. Letts and Sons. Autograph letter of 3 January 1863, containing approximately 25 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens thanks the stationers for "their annual present of an excellent diary."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 9 June 1838, published in Pilgrim I, pages 402-404.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 15 June 1847, containing approximately 240 words. London. Dickens offers Lewes two roles in the forthcoming private theatrical: host in The Merry Wives of Windsor and Old Knowelle in Every Man in His Humour . He then describes the organization of the amateur group.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 14 August 1859, containing approximately 210 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens warns of "an unaccredited agent," an American whose offers of profit are not the be relied upon: "a dollar in Wandsworth is worth fifty in New York."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 21 August 1860, published in substance in Nonesuch III, page 174. A fire-fragment with the complimentary close and signature alone surviving.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 6 July 1861, containing approximately 185 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens asks Lillie to send a glazier to estimate the cost of putting "the best plate glass" into specified windows of his house at Gad's Hill.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 29 August 1863, published in Nonesuch III, page 360.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To John Limbird. Autograph letter, with envelope, of 9 July 1838, published in Pilgrim I, page 412.

did.unittitle: G231: To Mrs. Lippincott (née Sara Jane Clarke)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Mrs. Lippincott. Autograph letter, signed, of 28 January 1868, containing approximately 60 words. Baltimore. Dickens thanks her for writing. He will be "delighted to comply with your daughter's request."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Henry Clements Lloyd. Autograph letter, signed, of 26 July 1850, containing approximately 105 words. London. Agreeing with Lloyd on the education of pauper-boys, Dickens cites, as a victim of an absurd method of instruction, the charity-boy in Dombey and Son , who is subjected to "a perpetual bruising of his intellectual shins."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Mr. Lonsdale. Autograph letter, of 17 July 1852, containing approximately 25 words. London. Dickens thanks Lonsdale for an "obliging note and its accompanying music."

did.unittitle: G234-G235: To Mrs. Robert R. M'Ian
G234: To Mrs. Robert R. M'Ian

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 16 January 1843, published in Pilgrim III, pages 424-425.

did.unittitle: G235: To Mrs. Robert R. M'Ian

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 23 August 1845, published in Pilgrim IV, pages 363-364.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Charles Mackay. Autograph envelope, signed, of 4 November 1869. London. This envelope is addressed to Mackay at "Fern Dell Cottage, Boxhill, Surrey."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 18 April 1840 [?], published in Pilgrim II, page 60.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 31 October 1840, published in Pilgrim II, page 143.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 20 November 1840, published in Pilgrim II, pages 145-146.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 20 November 1840, published in Pilgrim II, page 152. A fire-fragment with twenty-nine words surviving.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 11 January 1841, published in Pilgrim II, page 183.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 10 February 1841, published in Pilgrim II, pages 208-209.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 28 July 1841, published in Pilgrim II, page 343.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 5 December 1841, published in Pilgrim II, page 439. A fire-fragment with twenty-four words surviving.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 26 July 1842, published in Pilgrim III, page 287.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 14 August 1842, published in Pilgrim III, page 308. A fire-fragment with fifty-nine words surviving.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 16 December 1842 [?], published in Pilgrim III, page 394.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 1843 [?], published in Pilgrim III, page 617.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 4 September 1845, published in Pilgrim IV, page 371.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 1848 [?], containing approximately 45 words. London. Dickens is going to sup with Forster--"under the rose, for tonight I ought to dine with MacGregor"--to discuss proofs. He invites Maclise to join them.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 31 January 1850, containing approximately 50 words. London. Dickens makes an appointment to meet at the Blackwall Railway Station for the train "by which we must meet the boat."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 16 July 1850, containing approximately 30 words. London. Dickens invites Maclise to dine with him and Forster the next day; they are to meet at his Wellington Street office not later than five o'clock.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 16 September 1850, containing approximately 215 words. Broadstairs, Kent. Dickens asks Maclise to do "a little sketch of Mrs. Kitely's dress," a costume to be worn by Miss Boyle in "Bulwer's theatricals."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 21 December 1850, containing approximately 75 words. London. Dickens, who plans to walk in the country next day with his brother Fred, invites Maclise to join them.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of "Saturday," 1850 July 20 [?], containing approximately 70 words. London. Dickens writes that the "box is all right." He suggests possible times and places to meet.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 10 May 1848, published in Nonesuch II, page 89.

descriptivenote.p: G257: To William C. Macready

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 7 September 1853, containing approximately 90 words. London. In this "postscript" written on the inside of an envelope, Dickens thanks Macready for his "delightful note on the completion of B. H. --not the least of the joys of completing it." He reports that the 22nd of October is "the day appointed for Catherine."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 22 March 1856, published in Nonesuch II, paged 751-752.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To John M. Makeham. Autograph letter, signed, of 8 June 1870, published in Nonesuch III, page 784. It is claimed that this letter is the last one Dickens wrote.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Charles Manby. Autograph letter, signed, of 18 March 1861, containing approximately 60 words. After reading "the concise and terse resume," whose disclosures about the "Circumlocution Office" have not surprised him, Dickens pledges All the Year Round "to the cause of honest science."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To J. L. Manning. Autograph letter, with envelope, of 23 February 1842, containing 10 words. New York. Dickens writes: "J. L. Manning, Esq. with the compliments of Mr. Dickens." The envelope is addressed to Manning in Troy, New York. In spite of its epistolary form, this item is more of an autograph artifact than a communicative letter.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To R. H. Mason. Autograph letter, signed, of 20 April 1850, a fire-fragment with sixteen words surviving. London. In this letter, the text of which survives in transcription, Dickens criticizes a story about slave-owners in Virginia. "I promised to tell you the truth, and this is the truth, as I feel it."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To John T. Maull. Autograph letter, signed, of 10 March 1842, published in Pilgrim III, page 112.

did.unittitle: G264: To The Mayor of Reading

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To The Mayor of Reading. Autograph letter, signed, of 25 October 1851, published in Nonesuch II, page 355.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 13 July 1865, containing approximately 95 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens replies that he will not be in London before she leaves for America. "I thank you for the verses enclosed in your note. Many such enclosures come to me, but few so pathetically written, and fewer still so modestly sent."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 21 October 1867, containing approximately 50 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens is pleased to accept the dedication of her book. This letter was reproduced lithographically on the dedication-page of Infelicia .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Mrs. Metze. Autograph letter of 2 March 1838, published in Pilgrim I, page 381.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 12 December 1839, published in Pilgrim I, page 616.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 28 November 1840, summarized in Pilgrim II, page 159. The letter reads: "Devonshire Terrace. Twenty Eighth November. My Dear Mitton, I am bound to work tonight. Shall you be away tomorrow night? If so, what say you to Monday at 3, or 4?--I can come to you, if you like, at either hour. If this finds you at the office, send me word by bearer. Faithfully always, CD."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 13 April 1843, published in Pilgrim III, page 473.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 22 December 1846, published in Pilgrim IV, page 684.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Lady Molesworth. Autograph letter, signed, of 21 February 1863, containing approximately 55 words. London. Dickens declines an invitation for Sunday but hopes to see Lady Molesworth soon "and to receive an encouraging report of our neighbor's street door."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To George Moore. Autograph letter, signed, of 31 October 1859, published in substance in Nonesuch III, page 73, where the date 1858 is conjectural. Dickens agrees to occupy the chair at the next dinner of The Commercial Travelers' Schools [?], provided that the meeting is not held on the 23rd, 24th, or 26th of December 1859, or on the 1st of January 1860.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Emma Mordecai. Autograph letter, signed, of 19 March 1842, published in Pilgrim III, page 140.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Henry Morley. Autograph letter, signed, of 6 January 1849, containing approximately 130 words. London. Dickens thanks Morley for a book and offers to give a reading for the benefit of "people who can't read," but he refuses to become a patron of such a charitable society. "I do most strenuously protest against patronage." The book in question was probably Morley's Memoirs of Bartholomew Fair .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To J. Morris. Autograph letter, signed, of 18 July 1862, containing approximately 45 words. London. Dickens will keep the house at No. 4 Grafton Terrace at higher rent.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To H. J. Morton. Autograph letter, signed, of 27 March 1844, published in Pilgrim IV, pages 87-88.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Roderic Murchison. Autograph letter, signed, of 13 August 1852, published in Nonesuch II, page 409. A fire-fragment with thirty-one words surviving.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Sheridan Muspratt. Autograph letter, signed, of February 1852 [?], containing approximately 115 words. In this letter, the first leaf of which is gone, Dickens thanks Muspratt for his help. With the birth of another child, Dickens's offspring now seem very numerous to him.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To William Nicholas. Autograph letter, signed, of 22 September 1850, containing approximately 70 words. Broadstairs, Kent. Dickens was unable to call on Nicholas the preceding day and must now postpone the visit until his return to London in November.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To H. Nightingale. Autograph letter, signed, of 3 August 1869, containing approximately 60 words. Gad's Hill Place. Unable to find his copy of a book, Dickens sends his daughter's copy.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Edmund Ollier. Autograph letter of March 1864, published in substance in Nonesuch III, pages 381-382.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Joseph Olliffe. Autograph letter, signed, of 6 January 1863, published in Nonesuch III, pages 333-334. A fire-fragment with one hundred and thirty-seven words surviving.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To L. O'Sullivan. Autograph letter, signed, of 31 January 1866, containing approximately 90 words. Gad's Hill Place. Although willing to give three guineas to aid an "unfortunate gentleman," Dickens will not undertake an appeal in the Times .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 31 January 1839, published in Pilgrim I, page 504.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 3 August 1843, published in Pilgrim III, page 535.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Antonio Panizzi. Autograph letter, signed, of 14 March 1859, published in Nonesuch III, pages 95-96.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To J. E. Pfeiffer. Autograph letter, signed, of 25 May 1857, containing approximately 190 words. London. Dickens apologizes for overlooking a package with his correspondent's note and book. "I have just packed up the book to take into the country with me, and I propose reading it under the tree on the next sunny day." Dickens thought "J. E. Pfeiffer" was a man; she was an Austrian writer of travel books, whose A Lady's Voyage Round the World , published in 1856 in "The Traveller's Library," may have been the item in question.

did.unittitle: G289: To Monsieur Pitre-Chevalier (Pierre Michel François Chevalier)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Monsieur Pitre-Chevalier. Autograph letter, signed, of 18 January 1863, containing approximately 70 words. Paris. Dickens will be away from Paris for several days; when he gets back, he will write Pitre-Chevalier.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Mary L. Plumb. Autograph letter, signed, of 16 February 1842, containing approximately 240 words. New York. Dickens quotes the closing paragraphs from chapter LXXII of The Old Curiosity Shop , for the "fair with the good design."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Thomas Powell. Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 16 April 1844, published in Pilgrim IV, pages 106-107.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 25 September 1860, published in substance in Nonesuch III, page 181.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 31 March 1863, containing approximately 145 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens recommends "Rays from the East" as a title for her book.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To B. W. Proctor. Autograph letter, signed, of 19 November 1857, containing approximately 70 words. London. Dickens declines an invitation.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To William Pulford. Autograph letter, signed of 6 August 1843, published in Pilgrim III, pages 535-536.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Charles Rawlins. Autograph letter, signed, of 26 June 1847, published in Nonesuch II, pages 34-35.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To David Roberts. Autograph letter, signed, of 22 June 1855, containing approximately 100 words. London. Dickens reports he has the papers that Roberts forwarded to him from Mr. Howell for the defense of certain articles in Household Words dealing with the inspection of factories, which "have occasioned a private correspondence between me and a Manchester mill owner, one of the association--angrily begun on his side, and temperately continued on mine."

did.unittitle: G298: To Messrs. Robertson and Schroder

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Messrs. Robertson and Schroder. Autograph letter, signed, of 18 April 1849, containing approximately 65 words. Dickens declines an apparent offer for continental publishing rights to his works, "as I hold myself bound in that regard to Mr. Tauchnitz . . . from whom I have invariably received the most honorable treatment."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 7 July 1847, published in substance in Nonesuch II, page 45, where the date of 22 July is given to the letter.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 24 May 1848, published in substance in Nonesuch II, page 92.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To J. R. Robinson. Autograph letter, signed, of 16 July 1851, containing approximately 165 words. Summarized and published in part in Nonesuch II, page 329.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 23 December 1841, published in Pilgrim II, page 450.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 20 March 1844, published in Pilgrim IV, pages 79-80.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Mr. Russell. Autograph document, signed, of 3 October 1868, containing approximately 10 words. London. A cheque for twenty-three pounds payable through Messrs. Coutts and Co. to "Russell or Bearer."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To William H. Russell. Autograph letter, signed, of 17 January 1866, containing approximately 55 words. London. Dickens invites Russell "to come down from the following Saturday the 27th to Monday."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 30 December 1841, published in Pilgrim II, page 456. This letter is in the copy of The Old Curiosity Shop that Dickens presented to Salmon; see entry A60, copy 3. The Pilgrim text of the letter, being second-hand, is not exactly accurate; Dickens writes: "Here are the books which complete your set, so far. I was very sorry to miss you today, and will come to you tomorrow morning at Half Past Ten."
See Gimbel/Dickens A60: https://orbis.library.yale.edu/vwebv/holdingsInfo?bibId=4104991

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 1 December 1859, containing approximately 230 words. London. While Dickens is in sympathy with the aims of a group he does not name, he opposes the "public presentation" for reasons he will state to the leaders at a time they appoint.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Sebastian Schlesinger. Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 9 April 1868, containing approximately 70 words. Boston. Dickens thanks Schlesinger for a box of fans. "An official record" of "The Great International Walking Match" will be sent to Schlesinger. The "Match" broadside acquired by Richard Gimbel was Schlesinger's; see entry B303.
See BrSides Folio Gimbel/Dickens B303: https://hdl.handle.net/10079/bibid/4135983

descriptivenote.p: G308: To Albert Schloss

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Albert Schloss. Autograph quotation, signed, of 22 January 1844, containing approximately 15 words. London. On this leaf, one of two from the visitors' album of Schloss, Dickens writes: "And so, as Tiny Tim observed, God Bless Us Every One!" These leaves, which are summarized in Pilgrim II, in the fourth note on page 386, were inscribed at various times by twelve other men; John Leech's drawing of "Old Scrooge" stands out on the page where Dickens's quotation appears.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Thomas James Serle. Autograph letter, signed, of 17 November 1840, published in Pilgrim II, pages 151-152.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Alexander Smart. Autograph letter, signed, of 24 September 1845, published in Pilgrim IV, page 386.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 26 May 1857, published in Nonesuch II, page 849.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph envelope, signed, of an unknown date. London. This envelope, apparently sent by messenger, is addressed to Smith at "Egyptian Hall, Piccadilly."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Henry Smith. Autograph letter, signed, of 9 January 1852, published in Nonesuch II, pages 369-370.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Southwood Smith. Autograph letter, signed, of 26 October 1842, published in Pilgrim III, page 358.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Wyndham Smith. Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 9 March 1860, containing approximately 150 words. London. Dickens will bring Smith's letter to Miss Coutts's attention. He is pleased to know that Smith leased the King Street Market, being "much too good a man...for that association."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Frank Stanfell. Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 2 March 1849, containing approximately 50 words. London. Dickens explains the appearance of his correspondent's name in Dombey and Son : "The passage in question refers to a joke between me and my friend Mr. Stanfield, whose name, in Captain Cuttle's mouth, changes accidentally to yours."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 24 August 1844, published in Pilgrim IV, pages 182-186.

descriptivenote.p: G318: To Clarkson Stanfield

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 9 May 1845, published in Pilgrim IV, page 308.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 15 July 1845, published in Pilgrim IV, pages 332-333.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 17 September 1845, published in Pilgrim IV, page 381.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 6 March 1846, published in Pilgrim IV, page 515.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 28 March 1846, published in Pilgrim IV, pages 527-528.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 7 March 1854, containing approximately 105 words. London. Writing in seaman's lingo from "H.M.S. Tavistock," Dickens invites Stanfield to join him and Mark Lemon that night for a short cruise on the Britannia .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 20 May 1855, published in Nonesuch II, page 663.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 20 June 1855, published in Nonesuch II, page 673.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 19 October 1855, containing approximately 70 words. Paris. Dickens sends his address in Paris, "after the wildest and most absurd adventures in trying to get established under a roof."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 8 May 1856, containing approximately 25 words. London. Dickens writes: "A knockdown blow--I am desperately sorry, but there is no help for it, and I feel you do quite right."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 14 May 1856, containing approximately 220 words. London. Dickens encloses a letter about the Canada Railway to be forwarded to Mr. Johnson, and mentions a comical deception involving Stanfield.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 12 February 1862, containing approximately 110 words. London. Dickens will dine with Stanfield on 19 February, after which they will "see something" with Wilkie Collins and W. H. Wills.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 2 June 1862, containing approximately 55 words. London. Dickens gives directions for reaching Gad's Hill by train. Stanfield is expected there on 14 June.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 5 December 1862, published in Nonesuch III, page 323.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 18 December 1862, containing approximately 505 words. Paris. Concerned that Stanfield is ill, Dickens assures his friend that he will see him soon.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 21 September 1864, published in Nonesuch III, page 398.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 11 October 1864, containing approximately 45 words. London. Dickens will visit the next day to "form my own opinion of the lad with tarry trousers."

odd.head: General
odd.p: Volume also contains 12 drawings relating to Charles Dickens not included in Podeschi's catalog.

did.unittitle: G335-G336: To Mrs. Clarkson Stanfield (Rebecca Adcock Stanfield)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 9 April 1866, containing approximately 250 words. London. Troubled with "irritability of the heart," and in the midst of a reading tour, Dickens is kept from seeing Clarkson Stanfield.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 23 July 1867, containing approximately 360 words. London. Dickens reports on his progress toward securing employment for Mrs. Stanfield's son, Herbert, as a clerk at the Board of Trade.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Samuel Robert Starey. Autograph letter, signed, of 9 January 1852 [?], containing approximately 50 words. London. Dickens declines an invitation.

did.unittitle: G338: To The Stationers' Company, London

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To The Stationers' Company, London. Autograph document, signed, of 8 January 1844, containing approximately 110 words in the handwriting of Thomas Mitton; signed by Dickens. London. The copyright of A Christmas Carol was registered on this printed "Form of Requiring Entry of Proprietorship," which is filled out and witnessed by Mitton.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 27 July 1839 [?], published in Pilgrim I, page 572.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 17 August 1857, published in Nonesuch II, pages 870-871.

did.unittitle: G341: To Mr. and Mrs. Beecher Stowe (Calvin Stowe and Harriet Beecher Stowe)

descriptivenote.p: G341: To Mr. and Mrs. Beecher Stowe (Calvin Stowe and Harriet Beecher Stowe)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Mr. and Mrs. Beecher Stowe. Autograph letter of 3 May 1853, containing approximately 25 words. London. This invitation to dinner on 14 May, from "Mr. and Mrs. Charles Dickens" to the Stowes, is written entirely in the hand of Catherine Dickens.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Russell Sturgis. Autograph letter, with envelope, of 26 December 1851, containing approximately 50 words. London. Dickens thanks Sturgis and Charles Norton for sending a brace of canvas-back ducks, which "flourished exceedingly at yesterday's dinner."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Edward Tagart. Autograph letter, signed, of 20 January 1849, published in Nonesuch II, page 143.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Thomas N. Talfourd. Autograph letter, signed, of 12 January 1851, containing approximately 130 words. Rockingham Castle, Northamptonshire. Dickens reports that he has written to the "Fund people" about the Memorial." He thinks that if Sloane goes off bail, the law will be changed for that class of cases.

did.unittitle: G345: To Mrs. Thomas N. Talfourd (Rachel Rutt Talfourd)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Mrs. Thomas N. Talfourd. Autograph letter, signed, of 13 February 1838 [?], containing approximately 40 words. London. "Proud and happy to take counsel" with her, Dickens will call on Mrs. Talfourd the next day at four o clock exactly. The conjectural date of this letter was suggested by the Pilgrim editors.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To W. M. Thackeray. Autograph letter, signed, of June 24-27 1858 [?], containing approximately 10 words. This small fragment of a letter, which is tentatively dated, reads: "of any other person, in it or out of it?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To T. J. Thompson. Autograph letter, signed, of 19 August 1851, containing approximately 325 words. Broadstairs, Kent. Dickens describes "Charley" Gibbs's house at Quinto, in Italy, and advises Thompson "to take it on good terms."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To James Thomson. Autograph letter, signed, of 13 November 1837 [?], published in Pilgrim I, page 330.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Walter Thornbury. Autograph letter, signed, of 22 March 1870, containing approximately 80 words. London. Dickens is "proud and happy to accept the dedication of Thornbury's book. A copy of the work in question, A Tour Round England , in two volumes, accompanies this letter.* Dickens had suggested the subject to the author.
*Letter was removed from volume prior to processing. See Gimbel/Dickens H447: https://hdl.handle.net/10079/bibid/4230386

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 19 June 1858, containing approximately 35 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens sends "the autograph which you desire to add to your collection." Towle was then a student at Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 28 June 1868, containing approximately 65 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens will see Towle at the office of All the Year Round Tuesday next.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 13 August 1868, containing approximately 80 words. London. Excepting "the Paris subjects" and "Lucerne," Dickens takes Towle's pieces for All the Year Round .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 6 November 1868, containing approximately 65 words. London. While sending back a manuscript on vineyards and vintages, Dickens assures Towle that "The Peasant's Wedding" will be published in All the Year Round .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 9 February 1870, containing approximately 80 words. London. Thanking Towle for a copy of his book, Dickens asks his American friend to take home to New England "my affectionate remembrance of the country."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Thomas A. Trollope. Autograph letter, signed, of 15 November 1859, containing approximately 70 words. London. Dickens lures Trollope to write for All the Year Round by asking, "'What wrong has Pope Pius the Ninth done?' Don't you think you can very pointedly answer that question in these pages? If you cannot, nobody in Europe can."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To N. Trubner. Autograph letter, signed, of 21 July 1865, containing approximately 35 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens asks Trubner to read the enclosed letter and forward it to George W. Childs. The letter to Childs, published in Nonesuch III, page 433, is not present.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Louisa Twining. Autograph letter, signed, of 10 February 1860, containing approximately 120 words. London. Dickens agrees to help her cause, although he thinks the Poor Law is "infamously administered.'' There appears in All the Year Round "a little account of my visit to the workhouse."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Mme. Viardot. Autograph letter, signed, of 7 January 1856, containing approximately 50 words. Paris. Writing in French, Dickens flatters Mme. Viardot, calling himself "un des admirateurs de votre genie les plus sinceres et les plus constants." He and his wife will call on her Saturday.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Mr. Villiers. Autograph letter, signed, of 20 January 1860, containing approximately 195 words. London. Dickens sends the actor "A few words (from the Tale itself) to make a better exit for you at the point where you justly felt the want of something." The altered passage, from A Tale of Two Cities , reads: "And now, farewell Lucie, farewell Life! The world in which I have done so little--in which I have wasted so much--fades fast from before me; but in its stead, I see the lives for which I lay down my life, peaceful, useful, prosperous, and happy, in that England which I shall behold no more. It is a far, far better thing that I do, than I have ever done; it is a far, far better rest that I go to, than I have ever known. Farewell Lucie, Farewell Life!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To H. M. Wagner. Autograph letter, signed, May 20 1847, containing approximately 85 words. Brighton. Dickens declines an invitation because the maid packed no evening clothes. The Dickenses had intended not to "go out" while in Brighton.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To V. Wanoshocht. Autograph letter, signed, of 5 November 1858, containing approximately 50 words. London. Dickens replies that at this time he cannot prolong his series of readings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 5 May 1853, containing approximately 25 words. Summarized in Nonesuch II, page 460. The appointment in question is set for Saturday morning, 7 May.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 9 March 1861, published in Nonesuch III, page 212.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Herbert Watkins. Autograph letter, signed, of 10 November 1859, containing approximately 15 words. London. Dickens presents to Watkins this set of the Library Edition of my books." The letter is placed in Vol. I of the first set, entry D32.
See Gimbel/Dickens D32: https://hdl.handle.net/10079/bibid/1194166

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To John Watkins. Autograph letter, signed, of 18 October 1852, published in Nonesuch II, page 422.

did.unittitle: G366-G366a: To Mrs. Richard Watson (Lavinia Watson)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 13 December 1848, containing approximately 30 words. London. Dickens writes: "I enclose you the little book. It will not be published until next Tuesday. And I hope you will not like it less, when you come to read it quietly." The book in question is The Haunted Man .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 7 October 1856, published in Nonesuch II, pages 803-805.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Benjamin Webster. Autograph letter, signed, of 7 June 1855, containing approximately 20 words. London. Imitating telegraphic style, Dickens writes: "Telegraph Message. Tavistock Station. Thursday Evening 7th June 1855. 5:41. Charles Dickens. Perfect in 3 steps and covering himself with Glory."

odd.head: General
odd.p: Volume also contains a second copy of H149, newspaper clippings, and illustrations relating to Charles Dickens not included in Podeschi's catalog.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To J. K. Whammond. Autograph letter, signed, of 7 July 1862, containing approximately 135 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens finds Whammond's "publication" amusing and well written.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To I. A. Widger. Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 3 January 1851, containing approximately 130 words. London. Dickens writes the "upholsterer" that he limits his responsibility for the debts of Frederick Dickens to one hundred and fifty pounds, "which I paid (of course) on its being demanded."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 12 August 1856, containing approximately 185 words. Boulogne. Dickens comments on the articles in Whole No. 338 of Household Words .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 30 October 1861, published in Nonesuch III, pages 246-247.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 27 December 1868, containing approximately 80 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens, who "must try to hammer out an 'Uncommercial,'" sets Friday for "the audit."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To George Wilson. Autograph letter, signed, of 4 November 1856, containing approximately 85 words. London. Dickens thanks Wilson for a copy of his book, The Five Gateways of Knowledge . "I have read that charming little book with the highest interest and gratification. Wise, elegant, eloquent, and perfectly unaffected, it has delighted me." The book, a copy of which is in the Gimbel collection, deals with the senses of hearing, sight, taste, smell, and touch.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To John Wilson. Autograph letter, signed, of 18 July 1838, published in Pilgrim I, page 418.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Rebecca Wilson. Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 24 December 1857, containing approximately 15 words. London. Dickens sends "the autographs."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To W. Wilson. Autograph letter, signed, of 13 January 1839, published in Pilgrim I, page 492.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To W. F. Wilson. Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 6 November 1863, containing approximately 100 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens encloses a cheque for Wilson's "attention and good offices to my son." He is discouraged in trying Frank again at the War Office, "his failure being quite unaccountable to me."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To H. Wright. Autograph letter, signed, of 29 July 1862, published in Nonesuch III, page 301.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Elise Whitney. Autograph letter, signed, of 23 March 1870, containing approximately 55 words. Gad's Hill Place. Dickens thanks her for sending a "German account of myself and my writings."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To George Frederick Young. Autograph letter, signed, with envelope, of 16 February 1846, published in Pilgrim IV, page 500.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Marianne Young. Autograph letter of 16 February 1854, containing approximately 290 words. Summarized and published in part in Nonesuch II, page 542. Dickens closes the letter as follows: "It would be comparatively useless and hopeless to tell in a general didactic way, in Household Words, that soldiers' wives suffer such and such inconveniences. But an account of them, written like a soldier's wife, and presented as by a woman who had actually undergone them, would be quite another thing."

did.unittitle: G382: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph quotation, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph quotation, signed, of February 1839, containing approximately 140 words. [London?]. Dickens quotes the full lyrics of "Autumn Leaves" from The Village Coquettes . The piece, headed "A Song To Be Said or Sung about the End of October," is inscribed on ornately bordered paper.

did.unittitle: G383: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph quotation, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph quotation, signed, of 28 February 1842, containing approximately 135 words. New York. Dickens quotes from chapter LXXII of The Old Curiosity Shop , on the lesson in the death of Little Nell.
Box 21 contains a manuscript copy.

did.unittitle: G384: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed, of 8 September 1843, published in Pilgrim III, pages 557-558.

did.unittitle: G385: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph document, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph document, signed, of June 1845, containing approximately 15 words. Brussels. Apparently a leaf from a hotel register, on which Dickens inscribes the names of those in his party: "Douglas Jerrold, Daniel Maclise, John Forster, Charles Dickens, Mrs. Dickens, and Miss Hogarth," all from "England."

did.unittitle: G386: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed, of 4 March 1846, published in Pilgrim IV, page 513.

did.unittitle: G387: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed, of 31 January 1850, containing approximately 145 words. London [?]. Observing a rule of his, Dickens refuses to read a manuscript but advises his correspondent to try the public papers and the periodicals. He closes with the admonition that there is no "short cut or smooth road to the honors and perils of print."

did.unittitle: G388: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed, of 18 February 1851, containing approximately 90 words. London. While acknowledging a reservation for tickets, Dickens rejects the piece that his correspondent apparently submitted for Household Words ."It is perhaps best that I should avoid the subject."

did.unittitle: G389: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed, of 30 April 1855, containing approximately 35 words. London. Dickens returns a poem. "I think quite enough has been said and sung on the subject."

did.unittitle: G390: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph quotation, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph quotation, signed, of 2 January 1858, containing approximately 10 words. London [?]. Dickens writes: "You look very pretty to-day, Nelly; charmingly pretty." The passage is from chapter VI of The Old Curiosity Shop .

did.unittitle: G391: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter of an unknown date, circa 1860 [?], probably to his butler or cook, containing approximately 165 words. London [?]. Directions for a "whimsical supper," at which oysters were the main dish. In 1962 Richard Gimbel published a collotype reproduction of this manuscript as a keepsake for his friends and associates.

did.unittitle: G392: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed, of 9 June 1864, containing approximately 30 words. London. Dickens gives directions for reaching Higham by rail from Charing Cross Station.

did.unittitle: G393: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph letter, signed, of an unknown date. Only the complimentary close, "Faithfully yours," and Dickens's signature survive in this cutting from a letter written on black-bordered paper.

did.unittitle: G394: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph signature

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph signature of an unknown date. [London?]. A leaf from a visitor's album. Dickens's signature is one of eighteen on the page. Among the names present are those of John Forster, William Harrison Ainsworth, S. T. Coleridge, and George Catlin.

did.unittitle: G395: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph signature

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph signature of an unknown date. This signature apparently was cut from a letter.

did.unittitle: G396: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph signature

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph signature of an unknown date. This signature apparently was cut from a letter.

scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph signature of an unknown date. This signature apparently was cut from a letter.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: To Unknown Correspondent. Autograph signature of an unknown date. This signature apparently was cut from a letter.
