did.unittitle: Letters and letter fragments not included in Podeschi's catalog

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three clipped signatures, one original letter, and one copy of a letter collected by Richard Gimbel but not included in Podeschi's catalog.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series III is divided into 10 subseries: I. Books & Pamphlets, 1872-1874. II. Periodicals, 1825-1969. III. Printed Ephemera, circa 1866-1867. IV. Prints & Stray Plates, 1837-1968. V. Photographs, 1860-1868 and undated. VI. Mass-Produced Objects, 1894-1912 and undated. VII. Manuscripts, 1815-1966 and undated. VIII. Autograph Letters & Documents, 1772-1948 and undated. IX. Paintings & Drawings, 1838?-1916 and undated. X. Relics & Association Items, 1841 and undated.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: There are 12 sectional headings in this category. These captions, each of which describes a distinct species of collectable matter, appear in the following order: Books & Pamphlets, Periodicals, Sheet Music, Playbills, Printed Ephemera, Prints & Stray Plates, Photographs, Mass-Produced Objects, Manuscripts, Autograph Letters & Documents, Paintings & Drawings, and Relics & Association Items. Although the word "Dickensiana" is applied to works created not by Dickens but by other authors, every item catalogued here does relate to Dickens—in certain cases very tenuously. Within each species-section, the entries are introduced by alphabetically arranged main-entry headings, usually the names of authors and artists. The descriptive methods employed in this category are explained on pages xxxi-xxxii [of Podeschi's catalog].
Scope and contents note taken from John B. Podeschi's Dickens and Dickensiana: a catalogue of the Richard Gimbel Collection in the Yale University Library (New Haven : The Library, 1980).

Of the 12 sectional headings in this category, only 10 are represented in this finding aid to account for previously uncataloged materials. Only three sections (Photographs, Manuscripts, Autograph Letters & Documents) are represented in their totality--portions of the remaining seven sections are present. The remainder can be found in the Yale University Library Catalog using the call number "Gimbel/Dickens" followed by Podeschi's identifier.

did.unittitle: H187: Forster, John H. The Life of Charles Dickens . London: Chapman and Hall. Extra-illustrated copy

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Forster, John. The Life of Charles Dickens. London: Chapman and Hall, 1872 [Vol. I], 1873 [Vol. II], 1874 [Vol. III]. A uniquely extra-illustrated copy, with each leaf of the book inlaid on rag paper and surrounded by other loose leaves similarly inlaid and mounted with plates, original wrappers, title-pages, and leaves of text from Dickens's books, and photographs, newspaper cuttings, maps, cloth from bindings, engraved portraits of Dickens's family, friends and acquaintances, prints of places treated in the Life, and autograph letters and documents of men and women named in the book. The original letters and documents are catalogued individually in the appropriate following category of Dickensiana. The rest of the material, a rich body of pictorial Dickensiana, is not itemized.

dao.linktitle: Vol. I: The new buildings called the Adelphi
Vol. I: The new buildings called the Adelphi

descriptivenote.p: Vol. I

did.unittitle: H590: Gimbel, Richard, compiler. A file of approximately 200 miscellaneous cuttings from newspapers and magazines

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Gimbel, Richard, compiler. A file of approximately 200 miscellaneous cuttings from newspapers and magazines. Collected mainly from American newspapers, these clippings cover a wide topical and chronological range of Dickensiana: from advertisements for Warren's Blacking excised from London newspapers of the 1820s to an article in Finnish on Richard Gimbel as a book collector. Arranged chronologically, most of the cuttings are individually mounted on acid-free paper.

did.unittitle: H677: Pickels, John William, compiler. An album of approximately 175 miscellaneous cuttings from newspapers and magazines

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pickels, John William, compiler. An album of approximately 175 miscellaneous cuttings from newspapers and magazines. Most of these articles about Dickens were published in England between 1900 and 1930, although there are clippings here from Paris and New York journals and from papers published as early as 1882 and as late as 1936. Pickels, who lived in Yorkshire, usually dated the cuttings, many of which he collected from three sources T. P.'s Weekly, John o' London's Weekly, and the London Daily News . Provenance: Lewis A. Hird, who added to the album a few clippings from American newspapers and magazines.

did.unittitle: H746: Unknown Compiler. A file of approximately 80 cuttings from New York newspapers, covering Dickens's reading tour of America from December 1867 through January 1868

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unknown Compiler. A file of approximately 80 cuttings from New York newspapers, covering Dickens's reading tour of America from December 1867 through January 1868. Mounted on 23 pages of 12 leaves from a scrap-album, these clippings were collected by an admirer of Dickens who occupied seat 47 in row E of the first balcony in Steinway Hall at all four of the readings Dickens gave in New York City from the 9th through the 13th of December (four ticket-stubs were pasted in the album; two were later taken out). The articles--all of which came from five New York journals: the Herald , the Post , the Times , the Tribune , and the World --appear to have been assembled as they were published. Their content is summarized by Philip Collins in "Dickens in America 1867-1868," Dickens Studies Newsletter , Vol. IV, No. 2 (June 1973), pages 48-50.

did.unittitle: H940: Dinner Given to Mr. Charles Dickens on the Occasion of His Departure for the United States

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dinner Given to Mr. Charles Dickens on the Occasion of His Departure for the United States. [London: Charles Kent, Secretary for the Dinner, 1867]. Ticket number 3 for the dinner, which was held at Freemason's Hall, London, on 2 November 1867. Although there is a line printed on the pale green coated card for the signature of the "Hon. Sec.," a signature appears neither on this ticket nor on the six following tickets. -- A second copy: Ticket number 116. -- A third copy: Ticket number 232. -- A fourth copy: Ticket number 233. -- A fifth copy: Ticket number 234. -- A sixth copy: Ticket number 235. -- A seventh copy: Ticket number 238.

did.unittitle: H961: N[ational] P[aper] C[ollar] Co., Springfield, Massachusetts. A paper-board box for collars

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: N[ational] P[aper] C[ollar] Co., Springfield, Massachusetts. A paper-board box for collars. The manufacturer's printed labels pasted on the box indicate that it contained the "Dickens" style of "Bemis' patent shape, linen paper lined" collars, patented 13 February 1866. An illustration on the box shows that the paper neckbands got their name from the collars with short and rounded tips which Dickens wore late in life.

did.unittitle: H1006: Browne, Hablot K. 5 sheets of the undivided plates--with 8 plates to the sheet--of the 40 wood-engravings for the 1852 impression of Dombey and Son

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Browne, Hablot K. 5 sheets of the undivided plates--with 8 plates to the sheet--of the 40 wood-engravings for the 1852 impression of Dombey and Son published by Getz, Buck, and Co., Philadelphia. The first of these sheets, all of which are printed in octavo-format, contains the typeset title-page incorporating a wood-engraved vignette. Engraved on wood by Gilbert and Gihon, these prints are good copies of Browne's etched plates for Dombey and Son .

did.unittitle: H1019: Burn, Gerald M. A group of 7 etchings of buildings associated with Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Burn, Gerald M. A group of 7 etchings of buildings associated with Dickens, each print signed in pencil by the artist. The subjects are: "Charles Dickens's Birthplace, Portsmouth," "The House of Agnes, Canterbury," "Jasper's Gate House, Rochester," "The King's Head, Chigwell," "The Old Curiosity Shop, Lincoln's Inn Fields," "The Old Leather Bottle, Cobham, Kent," and "The Spaniards, Hampstead." These prints bear no date, but they were probably executed in 1924, which is the year tooled in gilt on the red morocco spine of the slip-case containing the etchings.
Slip-case discarded during processing.

did.unittitle: H1028: Charles Dickens. A small photographic print which reproduces a drawing of Dickens in middle age

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Charles Dickens. [Unknown city: unknown publisher, unknown date; circa 1870?]. A small photographic print which reproduces a drawing of Dickens in middle age. It is mounted on a round-cornered, brownruled card.

did.unittitle: H1059: A large photographic print of a drawing by Darley

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A large photographic print of a drawing by Darley which depicts the following scene from Chapter XXVII of The Pickwick Papers : "A plate of hot buttered toast was gently simmering before the fire, and the red-nosed man himself was busily engaged in converting a large slice of bread into the same agreeable edible." This photograph by Soulé was copyrighted in 1863 by Henry O. Houghton. The drawing is the model for the engraving by Hinshelwood that was published as the frontispiece plate in Vol. III of The Pickwick Papers in the Houghton-printed "Household Edition."

did.unittitle: H1060: A large photographic print of a drawing by Darley

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A large photographic print of a drawing by Darley which depicts the following scene from chapter XLV of The Pickwick Papers : "Vere do you feel it now, sir' 'In the same place, young man,' rejoined Mr. Stiggins, 'in the same place.'" This photograph by Soulé, copyrighted in 1863 by Henry O. Houghton, is from the same series of illustrations as the print in the preceding entry. The drawing is the model for the engraving by Hinshelwood that was published as the frontispiece plate in Vol. IV of The Pickwick Papers in the "Household Edition."

did.unittitle: H1064: "Dickens and His Characters." A small photographic print

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Dickens and His Characters." [Unknown city: unknown publisher, unknown date; circa 1870?]. A small photographic print, mounted on a card, which reproduces a drawing of Dickens surrounded by pagelike vignettes of characters and scenes from his novels.

did.unittitle: H1070: Fildes, S[amuel] Luke. "The Empty Chair, Gad's Hill--Ninth of June 1870"

descriptivenote.p: H1070: Fildes, S[amuel] Luke. "The Empty Chair, Gad's Hill--Ninth of June 1870"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fildes, S[amuel] Luke. "The Empty Chair, Gad's Hill--Ninth of June 1870." This large wood-engraving, which is printed on a sheet of plate paper 44 cm. x 62 cm., depicts Dickens's study at Gad's Hill on the day of his death. It is hand colored. Richard Gimbel owned Fildes's original sketch of the scene; see entry H1797. -- A second copy: Another impression. The typefae in the title is altered. This copy is not colored.

did.unittitle: H1076: Forbes, Cecil. An etching of the "Old Curiosity Shop"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Forbes, Cecil. An etching of the "Old Curiosity Shop," signed in pencil by the artist. The age of the print is difficult to estimate, although circa 1920 seems possible.

did.unittitle: H1092: Johnson, Thomas. A dry-point engraving of Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Johnson, Thomas. A dry-point engraving of Dickens, apparently based on a photographic portrait. This print on India paper is signed in pencil by the artist, who flourished circa 1900.

did.unittitle: H1097: Lange, Arthur. ["Dickens at a Reading"]. An etching

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lange, Arthur. ["Dickens at a Reading"]. An etching made in 1968 by a schoolboy. Signed and numbered in pencil, being number 64 of an edition of 68 copies.

did.unittitle: H1105: Lewis, E. Goodwyn. "Charles Dickens." Color halftone-photoengraving reproduction

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lewis, E. Goodwyn. "Charles Dickens." London: The Crown, the Court and County Families Newspapers [unknown date; circa 1900?]. This color halftone-photoengraving reproduces, "by the kind permission of B. Robson," its then-owner, the drawing Lewis made of Dickens in 1869.

did.unittitle: H1120: Onwhyn, Thomas, usually under the pseudonyms of "Sam Weller" or "Peter Palette." Illustrations to the "Pickwick Club"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: H1120: Onwhyn, Thomas, usually under the pseudonyms of "Sam Weller" or "Peter Palette." Illustrations to the "Pickwick Club.'' Edited by "Boz." By Samuel Weller. London: E. Grattan, 1837.

This is the third copy: "India Proofs," without titles. The wrappers are absent. Incomplete; contains 6 prints, being the illustrations to face page 46, page 111, page 137, page 141, page 177, and page 276. These etchings are mounted.

did.unittitle: H1132: After George Cruikshank. "Dickens Beside Himself (from Sketches by Boz , Illustrated by George Cruikshank)"

descriptivenote.p: H1132: After George Cruikshank. "Dickens Beside Himself (from Sketches by Boz, Illustrated by George Cruikshank)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: After George Cruikshank. "Dickens Beside Himself (from Sketches by Boz , Illustrated by George Cruikshank)." An etched portrait of Dickens based by Pailthorpe on a drawing in pencil made by Cruikshank in 1838, which is now in the Gimbel collection (see entry H1732).

Also present: Another issue, without the remarque-title and studies, but bearing the title, "Faithfully Yours Always, Charles Dickens"--being an etched facsimile of Dickens's signature. Issue on India paper, mounted.

did.unittitle: H1141: R[-], W. "In Memoriam: Charles Dickens, Born 7 Feb. 1812, Died 9 June 1870." Photographic print

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: R[-], W. "In Memoriam: Charles Dickens, Born 7 Feb. 1812, Died 9 June 1870." [Unknown city: unknown publisher, unknown date; circa 1870?]. This small photographic print, which is mounted on a card bearing the notice "Copyright," reproduces a drawing of an angelic muse-like figure and a weeping Britannia standing beside a sepulchral monument to Dickens. The drawing is signed "W. R."

did.unittitle: H1158: Stodart, Edward. An engraved portrait of Dickens taken from a photograph by Ben Gurney

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Stodart, Edward. An engraved portrait of Dickens taken from a photograph by Ben Gurney, New York. This engraving may have been executed in 1890 for issue in Frederic G. Kitton's book Charles Dicknes by Pen and Pencil . Gurney's photograph was taken in 1868. Dickens is shown seated, grasping his watch-fob and chain. Impression (pull) on India paper, mounted on paper-board. -- A second copy: Hand-colored impression (pull) on India paper, mounted on paper-board.

did.unittitle: H1171: Unknown Artist. A photographic print

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unknown Artist. A photographic print, mounted carte-de-visite style, that reproduces a drawing of Dickens by an unknown artist, who appears to have based the portrait on Poulton's 1861 photograph.

did.unittitle: H1172: Unknown Collector. A group of 281 prints mounted on large sheets of paperboard, being portraits and caricatures of Dickens collected from a wide variety of sources

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unknown Collector. A group of 281 prints mounted on large sheets of paperboard, being portraits and caricatures of Dickens collected from a wide variety of sources--from books, magazines, sheet music, and newspapers, with some of them being individually issued prints and in a wide variety of forms: engravings, lithographs, etchings, wood-engravings, photographs, photomechanical halftone- and linereproductions, and even a few hand-drawn copies. The collector's penciled notes prove that he was not highly literate; but he was thorough, for he found examples of most of the pictures of Dickens that were published during the author's lifetime. There are a good number of Dickens phantasmagorias here. In this assembly of prints are works of the following artists, engravers, lithographers, and photographers: Francis Alexander, J. C. Armytage, "A.B.," I. H. Baker, Fred Barnard, Janet Barrow, C. Baugniet, A. Barry, W. H. Beard, W. H. W. Bicknell, Bobbett and Hooper, J. Brown, J. R. Brown, Hablot K. Browne, W. Brunton, Alfred Bryan, R. W. Buss, J. C. Buttre, "C. P. & G.," W. H. CafFyn, Leonardo Cattermole, F. Courboin, George Cruikshank, H. Dexter, Alexander L. Dick, Alfred D'Orsay, B. Dondorf, Rose Drummond, "E.," Augustus Egg, Sol Eytinge, E. Finden, A. H. Forrester ("Alfred CrowquiH''), W. P. Frith, Harry Furniss, Andre Gill, Margaret Gillies, R. Graves, J. Greatbach, W. Gray, Ben Gurney, A. Halbert, H. B. Hall, H. Harral, S. Hollyer, Hooper, G. Sidney Hunt, W. G. Jackman, T. Johnson, J. D. Johnstone, J. M. Johnstone, Richard Josey, Theodore Joyeuse, F. G. Kitton, E. Landells, R. J. Lane, Samuel Laurence, Lee [?],John Leech, Lehman (lithographer), R. Lehmann, C. R. Leslie, E. Goodwyn Lewis, Henry Linton, W. J. Linton, Charles Lyall, James McGuigan, Daniel Maclise, Maclure, Macdonald, and Macgregor, Major and Knapp, Charles Martin, Mason and Co., J. Mayall, J. Kenny Meadows, William B. Meyers, Pierre Morand, Matt Morgan, Thomas Nast, Nichol, Thomas Onwhyn, "G. P.," "R. P.," F. W. Pailthorpe, R. B. Parkes, George E. Perine, G. J. Pinwell, F. Poublon, Poulton, D. J. Pound, T. A. Prior, T. Proctor, Jacques Reich, W. Reynolds, William C. Robertson, Edwin RofFe, S. Rogers, Joseph F. Sabin, Ary Scheffer, Thomas Sibson, J. H. Stanforth, J. Stephenson, Edward Stodart, Marcus Stone, F. T. Stuart, R. Taylor, Weld Taylor, W. M. Thackeray, A. Thompson, Thomas Unwins, Van Werneke, "J. D. W.," S. Wallin and J. W. Orr, E. M. Ward, Leslie Ward, J. and C. Watkins, H. G. Webb, A. Weger, Welch and Walter, L. Wells, F. Whimpers, R. Whitechurch, J. A. J. Wilcox, E. G. Williams, G. A. Williamson, and T. Woolner. Several prints, whose artists have not been identified, are cut from the following periodicals: Every Saturday, Frank Leslie's Illustrated Weekly, Fun, Harper's Weekly, Illustrated London News, Illustrated Times, Judy, Ladies' Home Journal, Little Folks (photographic copy), The London Illustrated Paper (photographic copy), London Morning Leader, Mephystopheles (photographic copy), New Fork Herald, The Period, Penny Illustrated Paper, Punch, Queen, Reynold's Miscellany, Tailor and Cutter , and Tomahawk . The origins of 17 prints are unknown.

did.unittitle: H1173: Unknown Collector. A group of approximately 75 prints

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unknown Collector. A group of approximately 75 prints, many of which are inlaid on plate-paper leaves, and several of which are covered by typeset guard-sheets; 4 of them are folding prints. There are portraits of Dickens, of his friends and colleagues (among them J. P. Harley and Robert Seymour), and of his parents; engravings of his houses; caricatures; illustrations to his novels (mainly by Browne, Darley, and Seymour); and facsimiles of a few manuscripts. There are engravings, etchings, lithographs, and collotypes, and various photomechanical types of prints. Several of the prints are original plates from Dickens's books; others are copies.

did.unittitle: H1174: Unknown Collector. Extra-illustrated copy of Forster's Life of Charles Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unknown Collector. The extra-illustrated copy of Forster's Life of Charles Dickens that, as a book, is treated in entry H187, is also acknowledged here as a great assembly of prints, hundreds of which are mounted or inlaid on leaves of rag paper. The man who accumulated this large body of pictorial Dickensiana covered the subject well, but there is particular strength in two areas: engraved portraits of the people Dickens knew, and topographical prints of the places where he lived, worked, and travelled. All types of prints are found here, but engravings and photographs are most plentiful.

did.unittitle: H1175: Unknown Collector. 592 mounted cuttings of illustrations from an unknown edition of Dickens's works

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unknown Collector. 592 mounted cuttings of illustrations from an unknown edition of Dickens's works. These prints are line-photoengraved copies, about three-fourths the size of the original wood-engravings, of the illustrations originally published in the text of Chapman and Hall's "Household Edition" (London, 1871- ). None of the "Household Edition" plates appear to have been reproduced in the edition, which was printed on coarse, now decaying, paper.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens taken in 1865. He is shown in evening clothes standing by a chair, hands in his pockets, leaning against a table covered with books. Published in Photographic Portraits of Men of Eminence (London: Alfred W. Bennett, 1866), where the text about Dickens occupies pages 93-99. This photograph is accompanied by the 4 leaves of text from the aforenamed book. The following three photographs were obviously taken during the same session, although Kitton ascribes them to Mason and Co., who, it seems, originally, published them, and with whom Edwards may have been associated.

did.unittitle: H1177: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown seated, in a three-quarter view

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown seated, in a three-quarter view. His hands are folded in his lap; his left leg is crossed over his right leg. The back of this carte-de-visite bears the imprint of the London Stereoscopic and Photographic Co.

did.unittitle: H1178: A photographic copy of a photograph of Dickens, who is shown in the same pose as above, except from a slightly wider field of view

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photographic copy of a photograph of Dickens, who is shown in the same pose as above, except from a slightly wider field of view.

did.unittitle: H1179: A photograph of Dickens, who is seated in full view next to a table covered with books; he holds a piece of paper that he appears to be reading

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens, who is seated in full view next to a table covered with books; he holds a piece of paper that he appears to be reading. This carte-de-visite bears the imprint of Albert E. Coe, Norwich.

did.unittitle: H1180-H1187: Gurney, J., and Son (Ben Gurney), New York, Photographers
H1180: A large oval photograph of Dickens (the image being about 22 cm. high), showing the head and chest from the right side

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A large oval photograph of Dickens (the image being about 22 cm. high), showing the head and chest from the right side. He wears a pleated shirt and velvet vest. Although this portrait bears neither title nor imprint, the majority of Gurney's other photographs of Dickens carry the copyright date of 1867, all of them apparently having been taken during one session in December 1867.
Item not located at time of processing.

did.unittitle: H1181: A smaller photograph of the preceding view (the image being about 7 mm. high)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A smaller photograph of the preceding view (the image being about 7 mm. high). This photograph is rectangular.

did.unittitle: H1182: Yet a smaller photograph of the preceding view (the image being about 5 mm. high)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Charles Dickens." Yet a smaller photograph of the preceding view (the image being about 5 mm. high). This carte-de-visite was published by the London Stereoscopic and Photographic Co.

did.unittitle: H1183: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown standing, with a top-hat in his right hand, and his left hand in his pocket

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Charles Dickens." A photograph of Dickens, who is shown standing, with a top-hat in his right hand, and his left hand in his pocket. This particular "Carte Imperiale," published by Gurney, is signed and dated on the back by Dickens, New York, 21 April 1868.

did.unittitle: H1184: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown seated at a writing desk, holding what appears to be a pencil

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown seated at a writing desk, holding what appears to be a pencil. An alabaster statue sits atop the desk. This particular photograph, a carte imperiale published by Gurney, is inscribed by George Dolby to Charles Kent.

did.unittitle: H1185: A stereopticon photograph of Dickens, who is shown standing, his right arm propped on his waist, his left arm resting on the back of a chair

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A stereopticon photograph of Dickens, who is shown standing, his right arm propped on his waist, his left arm resting on the back of a chair. This photograph, mounted on a stereopticon-card, was published by Gurney.

did.unittitle: H1186: A photographic copy of a "Carte Imperiale" photograph of Dickens, giving a monoscopic view of the subject in the same pose as he holds in the preceding stereopticon portrait

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photographic copy of a "Carte Imperiale" photograph of Dickens, giving a monoscopic view of the subject in the same pose as he holds in the preceding stereopticon portrait.

did.unittitle: H1187: A photographic copy of a photograph of Dickens in pose that varies only slightly from the stereopticon stance (entry H1185)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photographic copy of a photograph of Dickens in pose that varies only slightly from the stereopticon stance (entry H1185). His right fist is not visible here. This photograph is mounted carte-devisite style.

did.unittitle: H1188: Lock and Whitfield, London, Photographers. A photograph of Frederick Dickens

descriptivenote.p: H1188: Lock and Whitfield, London, Photographers. A photograph of Frederick Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lock and Whitfield, London, Photographers. A photograph of Frederick Dickens, taken circa 1867. He is seated with his legs crossed, reading from a book that sits on a pedestal. This carte-de-visite bears the photographers' imprint.

did.unittitle: H1189: An oval-vignette photograph of Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An oval-vignette photograph of Dickens, taken in 1865. He wears a threepiece suit of what appears to be a grey-tweed fabric; he sits with his right arm resting on a table, his head leaning on his right hand. This carte-devisite bears the imprint of Mason and Co. -- A second copy: A later issue.

did.unittitle: H1190: A photograph of Dickens dressed as above, but wearing a bowler hat. He is shown seated, with a large dog ("Turk") at his right hand

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens dressed as above, but wearing a bowler hat. He is shown seated, with a large dog ("Turk") at his right hand. This photograph appears to have been taken on the same day in 1865 as were entries H1189 and H1191-H1193. This carte-de-visite-size photograph is mounted on a piece of stiff paper, the back of which is occupied by a photograph of a young woman who cannot be identified.

did.unittitle: H1191: An 18 cm. x 23 cm. photograph of Dickens, who is shown amidst a group (Henry F. Chorley, Kate Dickens, Mamie Dickens, Charles Collins, and Georgina Hogarth) on the front porch of the Gad's Hill house

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An 18 cm. x 23 cm. photograph of Dickens, who is shown amidst a group (Henry F. Chorley, Kate Dickens, Mamie Dickens, Charles Collins, and Georgina Hogarth) on the front porch of the Gad's Hill house. Holding a glass of wine, Dickens jauntily leans against a column of the porch. Mounted on stiff paper, this particular photograph, which shows over half of the front of the Gad's Hill house, is inscribed by Dickens to Mrs. Childs.

did.unittitle: H1192: A collotype [?] copy of a photograph of Dickens, who is shown amidst the same group, and in a similar pose, as in the preceding photograph

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A collotype [?] copy of a photograph of Dickens, who is shown amidst the same group, and in a similar pose, as in the preceding photograph. In this view, which is confined to the porch and steps of the house, Dickens no longer holds a wine glass in his right hand; that hand is now placed in his pocket. Although this item may in reality be a photograph, it has the character of a photo-gelatin print. It is mounted on paper-board, on the back of which the following is written: "Given to me by Mr. Tamburini (whose brother-in-law knew C. Dickens) and he gave this photo to Mr. Tamburini's brother-in-law. March 21st 1906."

did.unittitle: H1193: A photograph of Charles Collins, who is shown sitting on the steps of the front porch of the Gad's Hill house

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Charles Collins, who is shown sitting on the steps of the front porch of the Gad's Hill house. This carte-de-visite bears the imprint of Mason and Co.

did.unittitle: H1194: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown wearing a frock coat with velvet collar; he is seated at an ornately carved table, with quill in hand; a sheet of writing-paper lies on the slant-board before him

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown wearing a frock coat with velvet collar; he is seated at an ornately carved table, with quill in hand; a sheet of writing-paper lies on the slant-board before him. The photograph appears to have been taken on the same day in 1867 as were the photographs in the following two entries. This carte-de-visite bears the imprint of Mason and Co.

did.unittitle: H1195: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown seated at the same table as in the preceding photograph; he reads a book while resting his head on his left arm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown seated at the same table as in the preceding photograph; he reads a book while resting his head on his left arm. This carte-de-visite bears the photographer's imprint. -- A second copy: A later issue.

did.unittitle: H1196: A photographic copy of an oval-vignette photograph of Dickens, showing his full face and chest

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photographic copy of an oval-vignette photograph of Dickens, showing his full face and chest. He is dressed as in the two preceding poses. This photograph is mounted carte-de-visite style.

did.unittitle: H1197: A photograph of Henry Fielding Dickens, who is shown standing with his right hand resting on the corner of an ornately carved table; he wears striped trousers

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Henry Fielding Dickens, who is shown standing with his right hand resting on the corner of an ornately carved table; he wears striped trousers. This photograph may have been taken in 1867. A carte-de-visite, it bears the imprint of Mason and Co.

did.unittitle: H1198: A photograph of Dickens, showing his head--in oval, cameo-style views--from four different angles

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens, showing his head--in oval, cameo-style views--from four different angles. Each of these miniature portraits is raised in relief on this carte-de-visite, perhaps so that each one could be easily cut out and placed in a locket. The photograph, presumably made in 1867, bears the imprint of Mason and Co. This particular carte-de-visite is signed in ink by Dickens. It is reproduced as the frontispiece of this catalogue.

did.unittitle: H1199: A photograph of Dickens, taken from the left side, with his head turned full-face toward the camera

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens, taken from the left side, with his head turned full-face toward the camera. Only his head, and collar, and lapels are to be seen. The photograph presumably was taken in 1867. This carte-de-visite bears the imprint of Mason and Co.

did.unittitle: H1200: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown seated in an open carriage with three women (his daughters Katie and Mamie, and Georgina Hogarth?)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown seated in an open carriage with three women (his daughters Katie and Mamie, and Georgina Hogarth?). The scene is the stable-yard at Gad's Hill. The photograph presumably was taken in 1867. This carte-de-visite bears the imprint of Mason and Co.

did.unittitle: H1201: Mayall, John, London, Photographer. A photograph of Catherine Hogarth Dickens, who is shown standing, with her blackgloved right hand resting on the back of a chair

descriptivenote.p: H1201: Mayall, John, London, Photographer. A photograph of Catherine Hogarth Dickens, who is shown standing, with her blackgloved right hand resting on the back of a chair

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Mayall, John, London, Photographer. A photograph of Catherine Hogarth Dickens, who is shown standing, with her blackgloved right hand resting on the back of a chair. The date of the photograph is unknowm; circa 1860 is suggested. This carte-de-visite bears the photographer's imprint.

did.unittitle: H1202: Naudin and Co., London, Photographers. A photograph of Dickens, who is shown reclining on the lawn in front of the Gad's Hill house, amidst a group of five men, four women, and three dogs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Naudin and Co., London, Photographers. A photograph of Dickens, who is shown reclining on the lawn in front of the Gad's Hill house, amidst a group of five men, four women, and three dogs. Dickens wears the grey-tweed suit and bowler that he wore for a series of Mason and Co. photographs (see entries H1189-H1193), which suggests that this photograph may have been taken in 1865 by a Mason and Co. photographer. It was published in Naudin's Portfolio. The photograph is mounted carte-de-visite style.

did.unittitle: H1203: Watkins, Herbert, London, Photographer. An oval photograph of Alfred Lamert Dickens

descriptivenote.p: H1203: Watkins, Herbert, London, Photographer. An oval photograph of Alfred Lamert Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Watkins, Herbert, London, Photographer. An oval photograph of Alfred Lamert Dickens, taken in 1860. This carte-de-visite bears the imprint of the photographer.

did.unittitle: H1204-H1211: Watkins, John and Charles, London, Photographers
H1204: An oval-vignette photograph of Dickens, whose head and shoulders are shown from the left side; his right hand is held to his cheek

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An oval-vignette photograph of Dickens, whose head and shoulders are shown from the left side; his right hand is held to his cheek. This photograph, which was taken in 1860 and which is the most widely reproduced portrait of Dickens, is mounted carte-de-visite style.

did.unittitle: H1205: A photograph of Dickens in the same pose as above, but enlarged and printed rectangularly

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens in the same pose as above, but enlarged and printed rectangularly. It is mounted carte-de-visite style.

did.unittitle: H1206: A photograph of what appears to be an engraved copy of the Watkinses' 1860 photograph of Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Charles Dickens." A photograph of what appears to be an engraved copy of the Watkinses' 1860 photograph of Dickens. The engraving is so close a copy of the original photographs of Dickens in the familiar hand-to-cheek pose, that this carte-de-visite is treated as if it were a true photograph and not a semi-photographic hybrid.

did.unittitle: H1207: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown wearing a black bow-tie and a twill coat with lapels trimmed in black; his head and eyes are turned slightly to the right

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Charles Dickens." A photograph of Dickens, who is shown wearing a black bow-tie and a twill coat with lapels trimmed in black; his head and eyes are turned slightly to the right. It is assumed that this photograph and the following one were taken in 1861. This carte-de-visite was published by The London Stereoscopic and Photographic Co.

did.unittitle: H1208: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown wearing the same clothes as in the preceding photograph, with his head propped up by his right arm as he gazes off to the left

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Charles Dickens." A photograph of Dickens, who is shown wearing the same clothes as in the preceding photograph, with his head propped up by his right arm as he gazes off to the left. This view appears to be a close-up made from the Watkinses' original photograph that showed Dickens seated facing the back of a chair. The photograph here was published, as a carte-de-visite, by The London Stereoscopic and Photographic Co.

did.unittitle: H1209: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown seated in an upholstered armchair, holding a top-hat between his legs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown seated in an upholstered armchair, holding a top-hat between his legs. This photograph and all the following ones appear to have been taken during a single session in 1867. The photograph is mounted on a carte-de-visite bearing the imprint of the photographers.

did.unittitle: H1210: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown in almost exactly the same pose as above, except that he now holds a cane and gloves in his left hand

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown in almost exactly the same pose as above, except that he now holds a cane and gloves in his left hand. This carte-de-visite was published by the photographers. -- A second copy: Another issue. This carte-de-visite, published by the Watkinses, is crudely handcolored.

did.unittitle: H1211: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown standing by a table with a fringed top, with his right hand resting on a book that is placed on the table

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photograph of Dickens, who is shown standing by a table with a fringed top, with his right hand resting on a book that is placed on the table. This carte-de-visite-size photograph is mounted on stiff paper. -- A second copy. -- A third copy: Another issue. This "Charles Dickens" carte-de-visite was published by The London Stereoscopic and Photographic Co.

did.unittitle: H1212-H1213: Bollans, E., and Co., Leamington
H1212: A pictorial bookmark-ribbon of woven silk (green, purple, yellow, red, and black on a white background), which portrays Dickens and his Gad's Hill house

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Charles Dickens, Born Feb. 7th 1812, Died June 9th 1870, Buried in Westminster Abbey, June 14th 1870." A pictorial bookmark-ribbon of woven silk (green, purple, yellow, red, and black on a white background), which portrays Dickens and his Gad's Hill house. A pious passage from "Dickens' last letter" is quoted at the foot of this ribbon. The portrait of Dickens is based on Ben Gurney's 1867 photograph of the author. The weaving technique is the kind employed in the popular Stevengraphs.

did.unittitle: H1213: A bookmark-ribbon from the same loom-stencils as the preceding item, but without the vignette of the Gad's Hill house

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A bookmark-ribbon from the same loom-stencils as the preceding item, but without the vignette of the Gad's Hill house. Bright blue thread takes the place of purple thread, but otherwise this ribbon is woven in the same way as the larger one.

did.unittitle: H1214: Dartmouth Pottery, Devon. "Dickens' Old Curiosity Shop." A pottery saucer, about 17 cm. in diameter, with London's "Old Curiosity Shop" pictured in light relief, in brown, on a golden yellow underglaze

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dartmouth Pottery, Devon. "Dickens' Old Curiosity Shop." A pottery saucer, about 17 cm. in diameter, with London's "Old Curiosity Shop" pictured in light relief, in brown, on a golden yellow underglaze. -- A second saucer: A variant issue, "London" being inscribed on the rim of the saucer, instead of being placed beneath the image of the shop.

did.unittitle: H1215: A bust of Dickens, 27 cm. high, made of plaster of Paris

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Dickens." A bust of Dickens, 27 cm. high, made of plaster of Paris. This casting may have been modeled on a bust Dickens originally executed in 1862. The sculptor is not known. See also entry H1232.

did.unittitle: H1216: Goddard, R. B. "Charles Dickens." A bas-relief, plaster of Paris portrait of Dickens's head in profile, 21 cm. x 17 cm.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Goddard, R. B. "Charles Dickens." A bas-relief, plaster of Paris portrait of Dickens's head in profile, 21 cm. x 17 cm. This casting, which was copyrighted in 1894, apparently was inspired by J. and C. Watkins's 1860 photograph of Dickens.

did.unittitle: H1217-H1222: Lancaster and Sandland, Pottery-makers, Hanley, Staffordshire
H1217: "Sairey Gamp Entertains Betsey Prig." A white china squarish saucer, about 15 cm. in diameter

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Sairey Gamp Entertains Betsey Prig." A white china squarish saucer, about 15 cm. in diameter, occupied by a color decalcographic scene from Martin Chuzzlewit .

did.unittitle: H1218: "Oliver Twist Asks for More." A white china saucer

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Oliver Twist Asks for More." A white china saucer shaped as is the one above, occupied by a color decalcographic scene from Oliver Twist .

did.unittitle: H1219: "Micawber Introduces David to London." A squarish white china ashtray

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Micawber Introduces David to London." A squarish white china ashtray, about 10 cm. in diameter, occupied by color decalcographic scene from David Copperfield .

did.unittitle: H1220: "Sairey Gamp Entertains Betsey Prig." An ashtray shaped as is the one above

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Sairey Gamp Entertains Betsey Prig." An ashtray shaped as is the one above, occupied by a color decalcographic scene from Martin Chuzzlewit (smaller but otherwise the same as in entry H1217).

did.unittitle: H1221: "Oliver Twist Asks for More." An ashtray shaped as are the two above

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Oliver Twist Asks for More." An ashtray shaped as are the two above, occupied by a color decalcographic scene from Oliver Twist (smaller but otherwise the same as in entry H1218).

did.unittitle: H1222: "Sairey Gamp Entertains Betsey Prig." A white china oval candy-dish

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Sairey Gamp Entertains Betsey Prig." A white china oval candy-dish, 9 cm. high, 12 cm. wide, occupied by a decalcographic scene from Martin Chuzzlewit (smaller but otherwise the same as entry H1220).

did.unittitle: H1223: New Hall, Pottery-makers, Hanley, Staffordshire. "Mr. Pecksniff (Martin Chuzzlewit)." A white china cigarette-box

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New Hall, Pottery-makers, Hanley, Staffordshire. "Mr. Pecksniff (Martin Chuzzlewit)." A white china cigarette-box, the lid of which is occupied by a color decalcographic scene from Martin Chuzzlewit .

did.unittitle: H1224: "Charles Dickens in His Study, Gadshill." A colored bas-relief "Ivorex" sculpture of Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Charles Dickens in His Study, Gadshill." A colored bas-relief "Ivorex" sculpture of Dickens, who is shown seated in his Gad's Hill study. Ivorex appears to be an ivory-like ceramic substance. Although these copyrighted sculptures may have been individually carved from 16 cm. x 22 cm. slabs of Ivorex, it seems more likely that they were produced from matrices.

did.unittitle: H1225: "The Old Curiosity Shop." A soapstone sculpture

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "The Old Curiosity Shop." This 14 cm. x 17 cm. bas-relief of London's "Old Curiosity Shop" appears to be a soapstone sculpture. Copyrighted in 1900 as one of "Osborne's Plastic Sketches," it is treated here as if it is a matrixmade object.

did.unittitle: H1226: "R. Warren, the Inventor of Japan Liquid Blacking." A copper token

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "R. Warren, the Inventor of Japan Liquid Blacking." A copper token, 3 cm. in diameter, the obverse of which is occupied by a relief portrait of Warren. The central feature shown on the reverse is a bottle of the boot-blacking fluid.

did.unittitle: H1227: Royal Doulton, Pottery-makers. "Dickens." A white china plate

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Royal Doulton, Pottery-makers. "Dickens." A white china plate, 26.5 cm. in diameter, on the blue underglaze of which Dickens is portrayed, surrounded by the heads of eleven of his characters.

did.unittitle: H1228: "Warrens Blacking, 30 Strand." A copper token

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Warrens Blacking, 30 Strand." A copper token, about 2 cm. in diameter, the obverse of which is mainly occupied by the pictorially ornamented numerals "30." The reverse shows a rooster pecking at his own image that is mirrored in a shiny boot, this scene being encircled by the following words: "The effect of Warrens Blacking, London." -- A second token.

did.unittitle: H1229: Whitehead-Hoag, medalists. "Charles Dickens 1812-1912." A bronze medal

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Whitehead-Hoag, medalists. "Charles Dickens 1812-1912." A bronze medal, 5 cm. in diameter, with the obverse bearing, in light relief, a portrait of Dickens apparently based on J. and C. Watkins's 1860 photograph. The reverse is inscribed as follows: "To commemorate the one hundredth anniversary of the birth of Charles Dickens. Issued by the Dickens Centenary Committee of New York, February 7, 1912."

did.unittitle: H1230: Unknown Sculptor. A bas-relief, gilded brass portrait of Dickens's head and shoulders in profile

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unknown Sculptor. A bas-relief, gilded brass portrait of Dickens's head and shoulders in profile. This casting is attached to a red-plush-covered board surrounded by box-like frame. The sculpture apparently was inspired by J. and C. Watkins's 1860 photograph of Dickens.

did.unittitle: H1231: Unknown Sculptor. A brass figurine

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unknown Sculptor. A brass figurine, about 4 cm. tall, portraying Mr. Pickwick in the pose illustrated in the first plate of The Pickwick Papers : "with one hand gracefully concealed behind his coat tails, and the other waving in the air to assist his glowing declamation." This casting appears to be of comparatively recent manufacture.

did.unittitle: H1232: Unknown Sculptor. A bust of Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unknown Sculptor. A bust of Dickens, 20 cm. high, made of porcelain-like unglazed white china. This sculpture may have been modeled on a bust of Dickens originally executed in 1862; see also entry H1215.

did.unittitle: H1233: Atkyns, Samuel. The Chimes: A Goblin Story of Some Bells That Rang on Old Tear Out and a New Tear In!

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Atkyns, Samuel. The Chimes: A Goblin Story of Some Bells That Rang on Old Tear Out and a New Tear In! An autograph manuscript of 64 pages, on 32 leaves, of a 2-act drama "adapted from Charles Dickens' work" by Atkyns, the actor who appeared in the role of Trotty Veck when the play opened in London on 20 January 1845. Bound in greyish brown wrappers with a copy of the original playbill for the play, the manuscript is signed and dated (21 December 1844) by Atkyns at the beginning and end.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bentham, Jeremy. "Systematic Sketch." An autograph manuscript of 1 page, on 1 leaf. This short philosophical manuscript, dated 20 September 1815, was found in Richard Gimbel's extra-illustrated copy of Forster's Life of Dickens (see entry H137), opposite page 314 of Vol. I, where Bentham's name crops up in a letter from Carlyle to Dickens on the subject of copyright: "Nay, poor Jeremy Bentham and others step in here, and will demonstrate that it is actually our true convenience and expediency not to steal." The manuscript is typical of the tangential nature of the Dickensiana collected in the extra-illustrated copy of Forster's book.

did.unittitle: H1235: "Charles Dickens: The Pickwick Papers: Comparative Description of the Parts Represented in Certain Notable Sets"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Charles Dickens: The Pickwick Papers: Comparative Description of the Parts Represented in Certain Notable Sets." A typescript of 152 pages, which are pasted on cloth-hinged folding panels and bound in 19 cloth-covered cases. There are 8 pages in every case, with each case being devoted to the "points" in a single serial part—from No. I through No. XIX & XX—of The Pickwick Papers . The following eight copies of Pickwick are compared: the Douglas-Austin copy, the McCutcheon copy, the Coggeshall copy, the Harry B. Smith copy, the E. P. Dutton and Co. copy, the James F. Drake, Inc. copy, the Robson (London) copy, and the McGeorge (Glasgow) copy. The compiler of this work is not known. Richard Gimbel's notes appear at several points in the typescript.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: No Thoroughfare. The original autograph manuscript of 168 pages, on 168 leaves, of the 5-act drama by Collins. The manuscript is divided as follows: act I occupies 40 pages; act II occupies 25 pages; act III occupies 19 pages; act IV occupies 27 pages. There are two versions of act V: the first version occupies 26 pages; the second version occupies 31 pages.

did.unittitle: H1237: "Obenreizer." The manuscript prompt-book for the role of Obenreizer in the play No Thoroughfare

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Obenreizer." The manuscript prompt-book for the role of Obenreizer in the play No Thoroughfare . This manuscript of 49 pages, on 44 leaves, appears to be the work of a copyist, but there are numerous substantive changes made by a couple of hands, presumably by Collins and by Charles Fechter, who portrayed Obenreizer on the stage. The prompt-book is bound in brownish grey coarse paper wrappers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "No Thoroughfare." This item is composed of leaves of the printed text of the play No Thoroughfare (London: Office of All the Tear Round, 1867) sandwiched between blank leaves, and all bound in brownish grey coarse paper wrappers. The manuscript matter occupies only two of the blank leaves, with the rest of the handwritten changes, presumably Collins's, appearing on the printed pages.

descriptivenote.p: H1239: "Wilkie Collins"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Wilkie Collins." An autograph manuscript of 3 pages, on 3 leaves, being an autobiographical sketch of Collins. In the bibliography that closes this piece (which goes up to 1870), Collins writes that the play No Thoroughfare was "written in collaboration with Dickens and Fechter."

did.unittitle: H1240: Fennell, Charles A. M. "'The Opium-Woman' and 'Datchery' in The Mystery of Edwin Drood "

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fennell, Charles A. M. "'The Opium-Woman' and 'Datchery' in The Mystery of Edwin Drood ." A carbon-copy typescript of 28 pages, on 28 leaves. The title-page of this typescript bears the following imprint: "Elijah Johnson, Cambridge; Simpkin, Marshall and Co., London, 1913."

did.unittitle: H1241: Gimbel, Richard. Manuscripts, typescripts, notebooks, and correspondence relating to Dickens and to the Gimbel Dickens collection

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Gimbel, Richard. Manuscripts, typescripts, notebooks, and correspondence relating to Dickens and to the Gimbel Dickens collection. The main elements in this assembly of Richard Gimbel's Dickensian papers are: 1) albums of cuttings--frequently annotated by the collector--comprised of entries on Dickens that were excised from auction catalogues and booksellers' catalogues; 2) charts and notebooks on the comparative points that distinguish the various issues of Dickens's books, in particular issues of The Pickwick Papers and A Christmas Carol ; 3) photocopies of, and correspondence about, copies of Dickens's books that contain presentation inscriptions by the author; 4) notes, manuscript drafts, proofs, and correspondence about Colonel Gimbel's published work on states of the first issue of A Christmas Carol ; 5) notes, catalogue entries, and correspondence relating to the Yale Library's 1962 exhibition of 150 items from the Gimbel Dickens collection; 6) papers and correspondence on the controversy over points of issue in The Battle of Life , a bibliographical squabble that beguiled Dickens collectors from 1962 to 1966; 7) correspondence between Richard Gimbel and fellow Dickensians; and 8) remnants of the bookseller-collector correspondence assembled in the course of collecting Dickens.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Dombey and Son." An autograph manuscript of 4 pages, on 4 leaves, written in a fine, small script. This introduction to Dombey and Son was written circa 1900 by Gissing for the "Rochester Edition" (London: Methuen, 1899-1901) of Dickens's works. The publisher abandoned the series after only six novels had appeared; and this essay and the following one were held in limbo until 1924, when Gissing's introductions to Dickens's works were collected and published (see entry H208). This manuscript, signed by Gissing, is bound by Riviere in full morocco.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Sketches by Boz." An autograph manuscript of 4 pages, on 4 leaves. This manuscript suffered the same fate as the preceding item. It too is signed by Gissing and is bound by Riviere in full morocco.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Grimaldi, Joseph. "Grimaldi's Address." An autograph manuscript, in the hand of an amanuensis, of 2 pages, on 1 leaf. Grimaldi sent this fair-copy of his farewell speech of 17 March 1822 to the actress Frances Kelly, with his letter of 20 March 1828 to her (see entry H1407).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Howells, William Dean. "Dickens's Letters." An autograph manuscript of 14 pages, on 14 leaves. This essay on Dickens, being a review of The Letters of Charles Dickens edited by Georgina Hogarth and Mamie Dickens (New York: Scribner, 1879), was published in The Atlantic Monthly , Vol. XLV, Whole No. 268 (February 1880), on pages 280-282.

did.unittitle: H1246: Lang, Andrew. "The Puzzle of Edwin Drood"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lang, Andrew. "The Puzzle of Edwin Drood." An autograph manuscript of 9 pages, on 9 leaves, written in 1906 in reply to an article by Arthur Eckersley on the subject of The Mystery of Edwin Drood . Lang apparently sent the manuscript to Eckersley, who later published it in the "Book Monthly" column of an unidentified journal. That article is here, along with Eckersley's article from The Oxford Magazine of 6 June 1906, and with other articles on The Mystery of Edwin Drood --all collected by Eckersley, to whom this file belonged.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Macready, William C. "Fund Speech." An autograph manuscript of 4 pages, on 2 leaves, being entirely in the script of a copyist except for the closing sentence, which is in the hand of Macready. This speech, delivered at the 29 March 1847 meeting of the General Theatrical Fund Association, was published in the Proceedings at the Second Anniversary Festival of the General Theatrical Fund (London: Brewster and West, 1847), pages 12-19; see entry B180. Dickens spoke at the same meeting.
See Gimbel/Dickens B180: https://hdl.handle.net/10079/bibid/4129931

did.unittitle: H1248: Parrish, Morris L. "Collation of Pickwick Papers in Parts: the Property of M. L. Parrish"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Parrish, Morris L. "Collation of Pickwick Papers in Parts: the Property of M. L. Parrish." A carbon-copy typescript of 35 pages (2 pages of which are typed, instead of being carbon copies), on 35 leaves. The title-page of this typescript bears the typed imprint: "In the Library at Dormy House, Pine Valley, New Jersey." This item was given to Richard Gimbel in 1955 by the Princeton University Library, on the occasion of his speech there on the question of earliest state of the first edition of A Christmas Carol . The typescript is bound in green cloth.

did.unittitle: H1249: Shepherd, Richard Herne. "A Monograph on Sketches by Boz 1833--1836"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Shepherd, Richard Herne. "A Monograph on Sketches by Boz 1833--1836." An autograph manuscript of approximately 240 pages, on approximately 530 leaves divided into 3 volumes. The pages in these three notebooks are numbered in analogy to the pages in Vol. I and Vol. II of the first edition of Sketches by Boz (London: John Macrone, 1836) and the first edition of Sketches by Boz, Second Series (London: John Macrone, 1837). On the appropriate page Shepherd records variations between the book text and the original periodical text of Dickens's pieces: "all the omitted passages are supplied, and the original readings of all the altered passages are exhibited...even single words or transpositions being noted, if of any significance.'' This manuscript obviously would be of value to textual editors of Sketches by Boz .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Swinburne, Algernon Charles. "Charles Dickens." A typescript of 32 pages, on 32 leaves, corrected in pencil. The following passage is written in ink at the head of the first page: "In Oliver Twist the quality of a great dramatist or tragic & comic poet in prose fiction was for the first time combined with the already famous quality of a great humorist & a born master in the arts of narrative & dialogue." This essay was first published in London's Quarterly Review of July 1902; and after Swinburne's death, his publishers made a book of it: Charles Dickens (London: Chatto and Windus, 1913).

did.unittitle: H1251: Ainsworth, William Harrison to Samuel Lover

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ainsworth, William Harrison. Autograph letter, signed, of 24 January 1857 to Samuel Lover. All of the following letters addressed to Lover are to be found in Richard Gimbel's extra-illustrated copy of Forester's Life of Charles Dickens (entry H187). The novelist Lover's (1797-1868) correspondence; the correspondence of the politicians John A. Roebuck (1801-1879); and the correspondence of John Chapeman (1822-1894), editor and publisher of the Westminster Review, account for a majority of the letters in this mixed assembly of Dickensiana. Dickens is never the subject of these men's letters, however.

did.unittitle: H1252: Aldrich, Thomas B. to Henry E. Sweetser

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Aldrich, Thomas B. Autograph letter, signed, of 9 March 1868 to Henry E. Sweetser. This letter and all of the following letters that are addressed to Henry E. Sweetser or John Russell Young relate to the dinner given in Dickens's honor by the "Press of the United States." Sweetser was secretary of the committee that arranged the banquet, which was held in New York City at Delmonico's restaurant, beginning at 5 o'clock on Saturday evening, 18 April 1868. Aldrich writes that he cannot attend the dinner.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ballantine, James. Autograph document of April-July 1863. The secretary's final accounting for the "Testimonial to P[eter] S. Fraser." Although he could not be there, Dickens was toasted at this dinner, held in Edinburgh on 1 May 1863. For the occasion he wrote a letter praising Fraser; it was read at the ceremonies, "amid loud applause"; see entry B282a and entry G5. All of the following letters that are addressed to Ballantine, J. A. Fullerton, James Hannay, or to Fraser himself, relate to the dinner. All of them are collected in an album along with the printed menu and programme of toasts for the dinner; a carte-de-visite photograph of Fraser; a copy of the privately printed pamphlet. Complimentary Dinner to Peter S. Fraser, Esq., Edinburgh, 1st May, 1863 (separately catalogued; see entry B282a); the printed letter announcing Fraser's death in Stirling, Scotland, on 11 December 1874; and a manuscript copy--in Fullerton's hand, it appears--of The Atheneum's short obitary of Fraser, published 19 December 1874.
See Gimbel/Dickens B282a: https://hdl.handle.net/10079/bibid/4134992

did.unittitle: H1254: Bartlett, G. B. to an unknown correspondent

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bartlett, G. B. Autograph letter, signed, of 14 August 1884 to an unknown correspondent. Bartlett writes about his "Dickens Cosmorama."

did.unittitle: H1255: Bell, Robert to Octavian Blewitt [?]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bell, Robert. Autograph letter, signed, of 10 June 1857 to Octavian Blewitt [?].

did.unittitle: H1256: Benedict, James to William Cullenford

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Benedict, James. Autograph letter of 30 March 1846 to William Cullenford. Benedict cannot attend the 6 April 1846 meeting of the General Theatrical Fund Association. Dickens was "in the chair" at the dinner. This letter and all the following ones that are addressed to Cullenford have to do with the General Theatrical Fund Association. Cullenford was secretary of the Association, and the letters to him from Dickens and other dignitaries are mounted in a green-morocco album richly bound and ornamented by Sangorski and Sutcliffe. Besides letters, the album contains the separately catalogued manuscripts of "Fund" speeches by Dickens and W. C. Macready, and "announcements and tickets of admission to the dinners, for various years, from 1846-1872."

did.unittitle: H1257: Bentley, Richard John A. Roebuck

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bentley, Richard. Autograph letter, signed, of 27 January 1868 to John A. Roebuck.

did.unittitle: H1258: Berkeley, F. Henry F. to William Cullenford

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Berkeley, F. Henry F. Autograph letter, signed, March 1846 to William Cullenford. Berkeley agrees to serve as a vice-president of the General Theatrical Fund Association. He will attend the meeting of 6 April 1846.

did.unittitle: H1259: Bigelow, John to Henry E. Sweetser

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bigelow, John. Autograph letter, signed, of 2 April 1868 to Henry E. Sweetser. Bigelow cannot attend the Dickens dinner of 18 April 1868, but he eulogizes Dickens in this letter.

did.unittitle: H1261: Blanchard, Samuel Laman to an unknown correspondent

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Blanchard, Samuel Laman. Autograph quotation, signed, to an unknown correspondent. This manuscript of Blanchard's 8-line poem, "The Double Lesson," appears to be a fair-copy that the author sent to a friend.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 29 February 1868 to John Russell Young. Boker will attend the Dickens dinner of 18 April 1868. Young, who was an editor of the New York Tribune , was chairman of the committee that organized the banquet.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 9 April 1868 to Henry E. Sweetser. Boker sends a cheque to pay for his ticket to the Dickens dinner, but he is not sure if he will be able to attend.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 25 February 1868 to John Russell Young. Bonner will be glad to attend the Dickens dinner of 18 April 1868, "more especially as he [Dickens] is an old contributor to the Ledger " (the newspaper that Bonner published).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 18 April 1868 to John Russell Young. Bonner writes that he will be kept from attending the Dickens dinner, but he sends his ticket to Young "with the request that you will present it to some friend of yours who may not be provided with ones."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of February-March 1868 to Henry E. Sweetser. Bowles will attend the Dickens dinner of 18 April 1868.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 13 April 1868 to Henry E. Sweetser. Bowles is averse to making a speech at the Dickens dinner.

did.unittitle: H1269: Brooke, James, Rajah of Sarak to John A. Roebuck

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brooke, James, Rajah of Sarak. Autograph letter, signed, of 27 May 1853 to John A. Roebuck.

did.unittitle: H1270: Brooks, James to John Russell Young

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brooks, James. Autograph letter, signed, of 20 March 1868 to John Russell Young. Brooks cannot attend the Dickens dinner of 18 April 1868.

did.unittitle: H1271: Brooks, Shirley to John Chapman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brooks, Shirley. Autograph letter, signed, of 'Saturday' to John Chapman.

did.unittitle: H1272: Brougham, Henry P. to Mrs. Phipps

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brougham, Henry P. Autograph letter, signed, of circa 1853 [?] to Mrs. Phipps.

descriptivenote.p: H1274: To George Setten

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 2 December 1872 to George Setten. Browne sketches a caricature of himself at the head of this letter.

did.unittitle: H1276: Bryant, William Cullen to John Russell Young and David G. Croly

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bryant, William Cullen. Autograph letter, signed, of 2 March 1868 to John Russell Young and David G. Croly. Bryant cannot attend the Dickens dinner of 18 April 1868.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 26 September 1854 to Samuel Lover.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 8 February 1870 to William Cullenford. Buckstone crows that he has secured the Prince of Wales to serve as chairman at the next meeting of the General Theatrical Fund Association. He will send the prince nicely bound copies of the Proceedings for "Fund" meetings chaired by Dickens, Bulwer-Lytton, and other dignitaries, but "not Thackeray-as he vilified the Georges in his lectures on The Georgian Era."

did.unittitle: H1280: Buller, Charles to Charles Roberts

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Buller, Charles. Autograph document, signed, of 27 June 1836 to Charles Roberts. A summons to appear before a committee of the House of Commons.

did.unittitle: H1282: Butts, Isaac to Henry E. Sweetser

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Butts, Isaac. Autograph letter, signed, of 10 March 1868 to Henry E. Sweetser. Butts will attend the Dickens dinner of 18 April 1868.

did.unittitle: H1283: Campbell, John to the "Attorney General"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Campbell, John. Autograph letter, signed, of 22 December 1859 to the "Attorney General."

did.unittitle: H1284: Campbell, Thomas to Baugh Allen

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Campbell, Thomas. Autograph letter, signed, to Baugh Allen.

did.unittitle: H1285: Carlisle, George to S. B. Brenthwaite

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Carlisle, George. Autograph letter, signed, of 29 November 1859 to S. B. Brenthwaite.

did.unittitle: H1286: Carpenter, Francis B. to Henry E. Sweetser

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Carpenter, Francis B. Autograph letter, signed, of 27 February 1868 to Henry E. Sweetser. Carpenter will attend the Dickens dinner of 18 April 1868.

did.unittitle: H1288: Chapman, Frederic to Charles Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chapman, Frederic, head of Chapman and Hall. Autograph letter, signed, of 25 December 1864 to Charles Dickens. Chapman answers Dickens's charge that the publisher had stopped sending serial numbers of Our Mutual Friend to the Athenaeum "by your instructions." He also reports that he has written to Melbourne to end the piracy of Our Mutual Friend by an "Australian paper."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 27 February 1868 to Henry E. Sweetser. Childs will attend the Dickens dinner of 18 April 1868.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 8 March 1868 to Henry E. Sweetser. Childs send fifteen dollars to buy a ticket to the Dickens dinner for fellow owner of the Philadelphia Public Ledger , Anthony J. Drexel.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 16 April 1868 of John Russell Young. Childs cannot get to the Dickens dinner after all; Mr. Godey will come in his place.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 5 August 1857 to Charles Dickens. Chorley introduces Carl Halle, the musician with whom Dickens was to work at Manchester in staging The Frozen Deep .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 2 July 1863 to Charles Dickens. Chorley, exhausted by a busy schedule which he outlines, hopes to "get away for August, beginning, I think, with a few days' rest at Gad's Hill." This letter is accompanied by what appears to be a photogelatin (Autotype) print of a photograph of Chorley.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Colenso, John W. Autograph letter, signed, of 30 June 1864 [?] to Mr. Malthus. This letter appears to be signed "J. Co. Natal."

descriptivenote.p: H1297: To Thomas Atkinson

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 28 June 1880 to Thomas Atkinson. Collins refers to an autobiographical sketch published in "the volume called men in This Time ." The manuscript in question was owned by Richard Gimbel; see entry H1239. Collins also writes that " The Lighthouse is not published. As long as I kept this play in a manuscript, I keep it out of the reach of theatrical thieves.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

scopecontent.p: Dickens, Alfred Tennyson. Autograph letter, signed, of 2 January 1911 to B. W. Matz. He asks Matz to admit two friends to the "Dickens Lecture" at Clifford's Inn Hall.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 4 March 1846 to "Abby." She writes her friends about the autographs she is collecting for her; she sends Samuel Rogers's.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 15 January 1848 to William Cullenford. She writes that Charles Dickens has secured Sir Edward Bulwer-Lytton to serve as chairman at the next meeting of the General Theatrical Fund Association.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 1 December 1856 to an unknown correspondent. On behalf of her husband, she invites the man to "our Christmas Theatricals."

did.unittitle: H1324: To Edward B. L. ("Plorn") Dickens

descriptivenote.p: H1324: To Edward B. L. ("Plorn") Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 6 May 1873 to Edward B. L. ("Plorn") Dickens. A long letter from mother to son, telling of the death of Charles Collins and touching on other family matters.

did.unittitle: H1325: A fabrication of a letter from Dickens to "Barry"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: [Dickens, Charles]. A fabrication of an autograph letter, signed, of 3 January 1870 from Dickens to "Barry." The subject appears to be The Mystery of Edwin Drood : "I want to say a word or two about the covers and the advertising, and to consult you upon a nice point in the tale."

did.unittitle: H1326-H1330: Dickens, Charles C. B. (Charles Dickens the Younger)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 3 May 1869 to Edward E. Lewis. On behalf of his father, Dickens warns the plagiarist to stop sending tracings of published drawings to All the Year Round .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 14 November 1870 to Mr. Macken.

scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 2 February 1876 to Mr. Marshall.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 12 November 1887 to W. Hamilton Gilson.

did.unittitle: H1331: Dickens, Charles Walter to an unknown correspondent

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dickens, Charles Walter. Autograph letter, signed, of 6 May 1907 to an unknown correspondent. He submits an articles, "Charles Dickens As an Actor," and tells the editor that "I Have a group-photograph of the performers in The Frozen Deep , with my grandfather in the center, which I might get reproduced if you thought it worth anything."

did.unittitle: H1332: Dickens, Ethel Kate to Frederic G. Kitton

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dickens, Ethel Kate. Autograph letter, signed, of 23 April 1902 to Frederic G. Kitton. "My father was christened 'Charles Culliford Boz.'" This letter is written on the stationery of "Miss Dickens's Type-Writing office, (Type Writing and Shorthand School)."

did.unittitle: H1333: Dickens, Francis Jeffrey to F. Converse

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dickens, Francis Jeffrey. Autograph letter, signed, of 15 November 1874 to F. Converse. Writing from Canada, Dickens sends Converse a "portion of a letter I received (when in India) from my later father." That choice item is not present.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dickens, Frederick William. Autograph letter, signed, of 23 December 1845 to Charles Dickens. Excusing himself for "not being at rehearsal tonight," Frederick Dickens assures his brother that he knowns his part "perfectly."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of Summer 1865 to Percy Fitzgerald. A letter announcing the imminent end of the Gad's Hill Gazette .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 10 August 1904 to Mr. Haslam.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 30 April 1918 to an unknown correspondent.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 5 September 1932 to Charles Sessler. He asks the American bookseller what value he would place upon the manuscript of "A Child's Life of Christ."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 3 January 1864 to Frederic Chapman. She asks Chapman, the publishers, to send a copy of A Child's History of England to her at "the office."
See Gimbel/Dickens H1873: https://hdl.handle.net/10079/bibid/1251241

did.unittitle: H1340: To Edward B. L. ("Plorn") Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 26 September 1868 to Edward B. L. ("Plorn") Dickens. This letter and the following five letters from Mamie Dickens to her brother Plorn are amound the best of Richard Gimbel's prizes. The long letters portray the family life at Gad's Hill in an intimate way; there are vivid and strong vignettes of Dickens in the role of a loving father.

did.unittitle: H1341: To Edward B. L. Dickens

did.unittitle: H1342: To Edward B. L. Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, of 17 June 1870 to Edward B. L. Dickens. She paints the scene of her father's death. "Of all his children he loved you the best. You were truly his Benjamin, my Plorn."

did.unittitle: H1370a: Fildes, Samuel Luke to Lord Curzon

descriptivenote.p: H1370a: Fildes, Samuel Luke to Lord Curzon

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fildes, Samuel Luke. Autograph letter, signed, of 6 May 1912 to Lord Curzon. The artist tells of his role in illustrating The Mystery of Edwin Drood . "Charles Dickens told me on an occasion & under circumstances that left no doubt in my mind, that Jasper murdered Edwin Drood." Fildes writes that Edwin Drood was strangled with the double neck-scarf that Jasper wore on the night of the storm.

did.unittitle: H1371: Fisher, David to William Cullenford
